narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical level for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile, including your early and later school grades, LSAT score, family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time, and it calculated an 84% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits likely to pass and succeed in practice. The three biggest factors pushing the model toward this concerning prediction, in order of influence, were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 decile, and your LSAT score. Most significantly, your Year 3 decile of 1—far below the average of about 5.5—strongly signaled to the model a risk of bar failure, as lower rankings in later years often correlate with struggles on high-stakes exams like the bar. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also well below the typical 5.7 average, reinforced this concern by showing early academic challenges that didn't improve much. Adding to that, your LSAT score of 30—lower than the average 36.7—further tipped the scales toward predicting failure, even though your full-time status aligns with most students and your lower-middle family income bracket is common. While your undergrad GPA held steady at a typical level, these other academic indicators carried the most weight in the model's eyes, painting a picture riskier than for students who pass the bar. Compared to typical successful applicants, who tend to rank in the middle or higher deciles and score above 36 on the LSAT, your profile stood out as higher-risk despite some average strengths. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a modest background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted effort—but the model focuses on patterns from past data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or prep programs, and reapplying next cycle with updated evidence of improvement. Schools value persistence, and addressing these key areas could shift your predicted odds dramatically in your favor.
